Row 3 has [2,16,16,4], so moving left merges the two 16s into 32 while preserving the 64 in the lower row area. Goal is to increase tile value and maintain board control with empties. Candidate actions: left creates an immediate guaranteed merge and likely keeps space; right also merges the 16s but shifts the 64 to the edge differently; up/down do not create as strong an immediate merge and may disturb structure. Best sub-goal now is secure the 32 merge with minimal risk, so choose left.

experiences.intentions: Key state: 4 empty spaces, highest tile 64 trapped near bottom row, but no immediate large merges available. Need to preserve space and improve monotonicity while avoiding filling gaps awkwardly. Candidate actions: Left compresses rows but creates a nearly full board with little gain; Right similarly packs but risks disrupting structure. Down keeps bottom row fixed and can stack columns but yields no merges and reduces flexibility. Up creates one immediate merge in the top of column 3 (2+4? actually column 3 becomes 0,2,4,64 no merge) and column 4 compacts empties upward; more importantly it moves larger tiles upward into a more usable arrangement while preserving empties at bottom for future spawns. Up best maintains mobility and opens future consolidations.

Comparing outcomes, right consolidates tiles to the right side and preserves/open spaces on the left, which is generally safer than up/down with no merges. It also keeps bottom-right occupied and organizes board edges. Best sub-goal is maintain mobility and structure; choose right.

experiences.intentions: Key state: a 4x4 2048 board with 3 empty spaces. Bottom-right 128 is the highest tile, and the board is fairly cluttered. Goal: preserve mobility while creating merges and ideally keep the highest tile anchored. Candidate actions: Left gives immediate merges in row 0 (2+2) and row 1 (4+4), producing two merges and likely keeping strong tiles on the right/bottom reasonably stable. Up would merge the top row 2s but also drags the 128 upward, weakening corner structure. Right also creates the same two merges but shifts the 128 firmly into the right edge; however row 3 is already packed and right may clutter the top. Down appears worst because it moves the 128 out of the corner and offers fewer benefits. Best sub-goal now is to gain space via safe merges without disrupting too much; left seems strongest.
experiences.tasks: Achieve the highest possible tile in 2048 by merging tiles strategically.

Goal is to preserve mobility while creating merges and ideally keep high tiles anchored. Evaluating moves: down appears invalid because every column is already bottom-packed with no downward movement. Right would move the 128 into the corner, but it also breaks the useful 2-2 merge available in the third row and shifts several tiles. Up would merge the first column (2+4+2+16 no merge) and second column (8,32,8 no), so little gain. Left gives an immediate merge on the third row (2,32,64,2 no actually none) and keeps board compact; more importantly top rows compress left without disturbing the bottom-right high tile too much. Between left and right, left maintains monotonic structure with larger tiles to the right/bottom and keeps more control. Sub-goal now is maximizing safe mobility and avoiding a potentially dead move. Choose left.
